response:
Ladies and gentlemen, esteemed citizens, and fellow dreamers of a brighter tomorrow, today I stand before you not as a mere observer of our nation’s struggles, but as a passionate advocate for a vision that is both bold and necessary. I speak to you today in support of a policy that many may not yet embrace, but one that I firmly believe will transform our society for the better: the universal basic income.

Now, I understand that this idea may seem radical to some, even controversial. But let me be clear—this is not about giving money to those who do not work. It is about ensuring that every citizen, regardless of their circumstances, has the dignity and security to pursue their dreams. In an era of rapid technological change and economic uncertainty, we must ask ourselves: how do we protect the most vulnerable among us?

The universal basic income is not a handout; it is a right. It is a guarantee that no one in our country will be left behind. It is a way to provide a safety net that is not dependent on employment, which is increasingly unstable in our modern world. With automation and artificial intelligence reshaping the job market, many of our traditional roles are disappearing. We must prepare for this future, not fear it.

This policy would provide every citizen with a regular, unconditional sum of money, sufficient to meet their basic needs. It would eliminate the bureaucratic maze of welfare programs, reducing the stigma often associated with receiving aid. It would empower individuals to take risks, to start businesses, to pursue education, and to contribute to society in ways that matter to them.

Critics may argue that this policy is too expensive, that it will discourage people from working. But I say this: when people are given the freedom to choose their path, they often choose to work harder, to innovate, and to contribute in new and meaningful ways. The universal basic income is not a replacement for work—it is a foundation upon which work can flourish.

Moreover, this policy would promote economic stability. By ensuring that everyone has a baseline of financial security, we reduce the risk of poverty and its associated social costs. It would allow families to invest in their children’s education, to access healthcare, and to build a better future. It would also reduce the burden on our social safety nets, allowing us to focus on long-term solutions rather than short-term fixes.

I know that change is not easy, and I know that there will be challenges. But I also know that we are a nation of resilience and innovation. We have overcome greater
